sources.title: Regions, Availability Zones, and Local Zones
Using SSL with a PostgreSQL DB instance
Backing up and restoring your Amazon RDS DB instance
Importing data into PostgreSQL on Amazon RDS

claims.summary: AWS documents RDS as a managed service that supports PostgreSQL database instances.
limitations: No service instance or control-plane configuration was inspected.

claims.summary: AWS documentation describes RDS for PostgreSQL 16 capabilities, including TLS configuration for version 16 and later.
limitations: The exact engine minor version and London instance-class availability were not queried.

claims.summary: The RDS region table lists Europe (London) as eu-west-2.
limitations: Region availability alone does not settle backup location or international transfer obligations.

claims.summary: AWS documents encryption at rest for RDS instance storage using AWS KMS keys.
limitations: No key policy, rotation configuration or encrypted instance was inspected.

claims.summary: AWS documents that backups and snapshots of an encrypted RDS instance are encrypted.
limitations: No provider backup metadata or restored encrypted target was inspected.

claims.summary: AWS documents point-in-time restore within the configured automated-backup retention period.
limitations: No transaction-boundary RPO or WAL continuity was measured.

claims.summary: AWS documents snapshot and point-in-time restore into a newly named DB instance rather than overwriting the source.
limitations: Alpacaly target-isolation and empty-target checks were not exercised against RDS.

claims.summary: AWS documents SSL/TLS, CA validation and verify-full hostname verification for RDS PostgreSQL.
limitations: No certificate chain, forced-TLS parameter group or client verify-full connection was inspected.

claims.summary: AWS documents RDS DB instances inside an Amazon VPC with network controls.
limitations: Public accessibility, routing and security-group rules were not configured or tested.

claims.summary: AWS documents native PostgreSQL pg_dump and pg_restore migration tooling for RDS PostgreSQL.
limitations: Alpacaly extensions, roles, triggers and full export portability were not tested.

claims.summary: AWS publishes RDS monitoring integrations and operational metrics.
limitations: No Alpacaly alert route, threshold, delivery or on-call response was configured.

claims.summary: Published RDS material establishes managed service and VPC boundaries, but this review did not prove Alpacaly's separate application, migration, backup, restore and monitoring roles.
limitations: No IAM policy, database role, break-glass control or role assumption audit was inspected.

claims.summary: This candidate review did not establish the exact Alpacaly secret-storage and rotation design.
limitations: General AWS secret-management services were outside this RDS candidate evidence review.

claims.summary: AWS publishes monitoring capabilities, but this review did not prove complete immutable administrative audit coverage for the proposed design.
limitations: No control-plane audit trail, retention rule or identity-backed reviewer evidence was inspected.

claims.summary: AWS documents automated-backup retention and persistent manual snapshots.
limitations: Required daily, weekly, monthly, minimum-retention and deletion-evidence policy was not configured.

claims.summary: The reviewed RDS documents did not establish an approved legal or incident hold workflow for Alpacaly backups.
limitations: The existing Alpacaly hold registry still requires an approved provider-side operating procedure.

claims.summary: This review did not record the applicable London quotas, connection limits, storage bounds or restore constraints for the proposed size.
limitations: Instance-class and account quota information can vary and requires a later controlled review.

claims.summary: No decision-grade incident-history review was completed for this candidate.
limitations: A public status view is not equivalent to contractual or candidate-specific incident evidence.

claims.summary: AWS publishes support-plan descriptions, but no plan was selected and no response commitment was accepted.
limitations: Entitlement, response target, escalation route and cost require independent contractual review.

claims.summary: AWS publishes variable RDS pricing components, but this review does not contain an approved London architecture or monthly quote.
limitations: Compute, storage, I/O, backup, transfer, monitoring, support and tax assumptions remain unpriced.

claims.summary: AWS documents an isolated Europe (London) region, but this is not a completed UK data-transfer or privacy assessment.
limitations: Backup locations, support access, telemetry, subprocessors and contractual transfers remain unreviewed.

claims.summary: Published AWS documentation cannot prove Alpacaly recovery-safety mode, claim fencing, emergency-stop preservation or OUTCOME_UNKNOWN handling.
limitations: A disposable provider-backed restore drill is mandatory before any later staging evaluation can pass.

openQuestions: Which London deployment shape meets the proposed RPO and RTO without relying on untested assumptions?
How will separate application, migration, backup, restore and monitoring roles be enforced and audited?
What backup, PITR and telemetry data can leave the UK region under normal operations or support access?
What support tier, escalation route and response commitment would be contractually available?
What are the full monthly costs for HA, backups, PITR, monitoring, transfer, support and restore drills?
How will legal holds and deletion evidence be applied without weakening the existing append-only catalogue?
